content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 20, 1992


Honorable Brian Cochran
City of Lemon Grove
Office of City Council
3232 Main Street
Lemon Grove, CA 92045

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-401

Dear Mayor Cochran:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

This letter confirms that your letter dated July 10, 1992,
accurately summarizes the telephone advice provided to you, that
the Act does not preclude your leasing of real property in a
redevelopment area.

As we discussed, Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any
public official at any level of state or local government from
participating in a governmental decision in which the official
knows or has reason to know the official has a financial interest.
The private decision to lease property is not regulated by the
Act.2

However, once you do lease property in the area, the property
could be an economic interest which may result in a conflict of
interest. Section 87103 provides that you have a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 This letter does not pertain to other laws outside of the Act
that may apply to your facts. You should check with your city
attorney concerning other areas of law, such as redevelopment law.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-401
Page 2


decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on you, your immediate
family, or on any real property in which you have an interest of
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

Section 82033 provides that an "interest in real property"
includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest in real
property located in the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or
beneficially by the public official if the fair market value of
the interest is $1,000 or more.³ Regulation 18729(b) provides
that the value of a leasehold interest is the amount of rent owed
during a 12-month period. Thus, if your payments on the lease are
$1,000 or more annually, you have a leasehold interest in real
property as defined in the Act.⁴

Moreover, as we discussed, your clients are sources of income
which may also result in a conflict of interest. We have enclosed
information concerning sources of income for your information.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁵

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca Breeze
    Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BB:aa


³ A month-to-month tenancy is not a leasehold interest under the
Act. (Regulation 18233.)

⁴ We have enclosed some general information concerning conflicts
of interest for your information.

⁵ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from leasing real property in a redevelopment area and what conflict of interest issues may arise from such a lease.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Act does not preclude leasing real property in a redevelopment area, but once leased, the property may constitute a financial interest creating a conflict of interest if annual lease payments are $1,000 or more and the decision has a material financial effect on the official. The official's clients as income sources may also create conflicts of interest.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is FPPC response confirming prior telephone advice. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers, but content clearly contains legal question and conclusion. Document cites Government Code Sections 87100, 87103, 82033 and Regulation 18729(b). Recipient is City of Lemon Grove Mayor Brian Cochran, dated August 20, 1992.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from leasing real property in a redevelopment area and what conflict of interest issues may arise from such a lease.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not preclude leasing real property in a redevelopment area, but once leased, the property may constitute a financial interest creating a conflict of interest if annual lease payments are $1,000 or more and the decision has a material financial effect on the official. The official's clients as income sources may also create conflicts of interest.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice to Lemon Grove Mayor Cochran that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit leasing real property in a redevelopment area, but leasehold interests of $1,000 or more annually may create conflicts of interest in governmental decisions affecting such property.
